title: Delete account vs destructive action confirmation vs typed confirmation vs data export vs settings management vs restore from trash
summary: Use delete account for account-level closure and associated data deletion. Use destructive action confirmation for one destructive object command, typed confirmation for severe target verification, data export for keeping a copy before deletion, settings management for durable configuration, and restore from trash for reversible deletion recovery.
decisionRules: Choose delete account when the user intends account-level closure with associated data deletion, including removal or requested removal of associated personal, profile, authentication, app, subscription, and service data.
Choose destructive action confirmation when the target is one project, file, record, subscription, key, or workspace object rather than the user's account identity and legal/data-retention lifecycle.
Choose typed confirmation as an added gate inside delete account only when the account identifier, workspace, or broad data scope is severe enough that wrong-account deletion is plausible.
Choose data export when users need to download or transfer a portable copy before deletion; export does not itself close the account or erase source data.
Choose settings management when users are changing privacy, notification, profile, billing, or account preferences that keep the account active.
Choose restore from trash when the product can faithfully recover a deleted object or account within a clear recovery window; do not present irreversible account closure as a trash move.

A delete-account flow should show account identity, affected services, retained data, subscriptions or billing, linked sign-in methods, export-before-delete, waiting period or scheduled deletion, cancellation or recovery window, and final deleted/blocked status.

Do not hide account deletion behind support-only contact, an unrelated settings page, a toggle, an unlabeled Delete button, or a data export flow when platform or product policy requires a discoverable deletion path.

When legal, safety, fraud, billing, child-account, enterprise, or regulated-industry retention prevents immediate erasure, the flow should say what is retained, why, for how long or by what policy, and what request state was recorded.

When deletion affects third-party recipients, published data, linked apps, or backups, the flow should distinguish live-account deactivation, personal data erasure, recipient notification, backup overwrite timing, and recoverability.
failureModes: The account settings page only offers deactivate account even though users expect deletion of the account record and associated personal data.
Users delete the wrong account because the flow hides the email, provider, workspace, child account, or signed-in identity being closed.
A data export screen implies that downloading data also deletes the source account.
The product says account deleted while subscriptions, app-store billing, Sign in with Apple tokens, linked apps, or recovery email dependencies remain unresolved.
The flow claims all data is erased immediately while legal retention, backups, fraud logs, or public recipients remain undisclosed.
The account can be deleted only by emailing support even though the product has ordinary self-serve account creation and no regulated exception.

sources.note: Supports discoverable in-app deletion initiation, full account-record deletion, transparency, direct web completion, and billing guidance.
Supports in-app and web deletion paths, associated data deletion, store-listing disclosure, sensitive data scope, and retained-data expectations.
Supports user-facing review, download-before-delete, dependent-service updates, whole-account versus service deletion distinction, and recovery window.
Supports erasure request handling, non-absolute rights, response timing, recipient notification, backup handling, and legal exceptions.
